result:
I'll write about something that's been on my mind lately: the strange, quiet revolution happening in how we pay attention to the world around us.

---

There's a particular kind of silence that descends on a city around four in the morning. I've been chasing this silence for years now, not always successfully. It exists in the gap between the last straggling bars closing and the first delivery trucks beginning their rounds. The streetlights hold onto their orange glow, but they've stopped competing with anything. They're just *there*, doing their job with a kind of mechanical patience that feels almost tender.

I used to think this silence was empty. Now I'm convinced it's the opposite—so full that most of us can't bear to approach it.

We've built elaborate architectures to avoid this fullness. The podcast during the commute, the music during the workout, the endless scroll during every interstitial moment of the day. I'm not judging this; I do it too. But I've become interested in what we might be insulating ourselves from, what we decided collectively and individually was too much to handle.

The philosopher Byung-Chul Han writes about the "burnout society," where we're all self-exploiters in an economy of achievement. But I think there's something else happening too, something more intimate and harder to name. We're not just tired from doing too much. We're exhausted from never fully arriving anywhere.

Consider the last time you stood in a line without pulling out your phone. Not as an exercise in digital minimalism, not as a boast about your mindfulness practice, but simply because there was nothing else to do. What rose up in that space? For most of us, something uncomfortable. The mind starts inventorying anxieties, rehearsing conversations, spiraling through ancient embarrassments with the fresh vividness of wounds that never quite scarred over.

But wait longer—past the initial itch, past the compulsion to *do* something with yourself—and something else happens. The fluorescent lights of the pharmacy become interesting. The particular way the person ahead of you holds their shoulders. The smell of the rain that started while you weren't paying attention. The world, which had receded to a blurry background for your important foreground concerns, steps forward again. It was always there. It was waiting.

This isn't a romantic argument for simplicity. The world that steps forward contains plenty that's difficult: the man talking to himself in the corner, the advertisement preying on insecurity, the subtle hierarchies of who gets served first. Attention isn't inherently redemptive. But it is *real* in a way that distraction never quite manages. And reality, I've come to believe, has a kind of gravity that we need, even when it pulls at us painfully.

I've been thinking about this in relation to what we might call "deep hobbies"—pursuits that resist optimization, that don't translate well to social media, that offer no credential or career advantage. My neighbor, who is in his seventies, has spent thirty years learning to identify birds by their songs. Not to publish a guide, not to lead tours, but because the knowledge itself became necessary to him. He describes hearing a black-throated green warbler after years of absence as "like encountering an old friend who you weren't sure was still alive." This is attention accumulated over time, attention that has become love without ever announcing itself as such.

Contrast this with what much of our technology offers: the promise of attention without effort, interest without investment, connection without commitment. The algorithm presents us with exactly what will engage us next, and in doing so, prevents us from discovering what we might have found if we'd had to search, to wait, to be bored. Boredom, that state we flee from, turns out to be the threshold of something. It's the door that opens onto unexpected rooms.

I'm not sure when we collectively decided that the highest good was the elimination of friction. Perhaps it was inevitable, given our economic logic: friction is inefficiency, inefficiency is waste, waste is sin. But human lives require texture. We need resistance to push against, dead ends that force us to turn around and notice what we'd passed, difficulties that shape us in the overcoming. A life without friction is a life without form, just as water without a channel spreads thin and directionless.

There's a practice I've developed, more accident than discipline. Once a week, I take a walk with no destination and no podcast, and I try to notice one thing thoroughly. Not photograph it, not research it, not share it—just notice it. Last week it was the way light moved through the leaves of a particular tree, creating patterns on the sidewalk that shifted with every breeze. I stood there for perhaps ten minutes, feeling foolish, feeling present. I couldn't tell you what species of tree it was. I can't show you a picture. But I can still see it when I close my eyes, which is more than I can say for most of what I scrolled past that same afternoon.

We're living through what the novelist Richard Powers calls "the great derangement"—a time when our ways of perceiving have become so abstracted from the physical world that we can know *about* climate catastrophe without truly *feeling* it, can understand intellectually that species are disappearing while continuing to live as though the background will always be there, reliably scenic, available for our use.

What would it mean to recover a more embodied attention? Not as a retreat from the complex systems that shape our lives, but as a necessary complement to them? I think it would mean allowing ourselves to be affected, to be slowed, to be surprised. It would mean risking the discomfort of not knowing what comes next, of not having the right response ready, of simply being present without a purpose.

The four AM silence I mentioned earlier—I've started to seek it out deliberately, not every night, but enough to remember what it contains. The city doesn't stop at that hour, but it changes its rhythm. A single car passing sounds like an event. The distant hum of the highway becomes a kind of ocean. And gradually, the mind adjusts its own frequency, stops shouting over the noise it's used to, and begins to hear quieter things: its own steady breathing, the settling of the building, some animal presence in the alley that I never notice during the day.

I'm not sure what any of this means, practically. I'm not going to tell you to delete your apps or move to a cabin. The world we have is the world we have, and it contains genuine goods alongside its genuine losses. But I do think we might ask ourselves more often: what am I paying attention to, and what is that attention costing me? What am I missing in my efficient passage through the day? What might be waiting in the spaces I keep filled?

The black-throated green warbler doesn't care if my neighbor knows its name. But his knowing has changed something in him, opened a door that remains open. That's what attention does, when we let it accumulate. It doesn't just inform us. It transforms us. It makes us available to a world that's always been more than we noticed, more than we could hold, more than we deserved perhaps, but there it is, patient, waiting for us to look up.

---
